records.ety: [AS. nicor a marine monster; akin to D. nikker a water spite, Icel. nykr, ONG. nihhus a crocodile, G. nix a water sprite; cf. Gr. ni`ptein to wash, Skr. nij. Cf. Nix.]

defs: An evil spirit of the waters.
A score for keeping an account; a reckoning.
A notch cut crosswise in the shank of a type, to assist a compositor in placing it properly in the stick, and in distribution.
A broken or indented place in any edge or surface; as, nicks in a china plate; a nick in the table top.
A particular point or place considered as marked by a nick; the exact point or critical moment.
quotes: To cut it off in the very nick.
This nick of time is the critical occasion for the gaining of a point.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Nicked (nkt); p. pr. & vb. n. Nicking.]

defs: To make a nick or nicks in; to notch; to keep count of or upon by nicks; as, to nick a stick, tally, etc.
To mar; to deface; to make ragged, as by cutting nicks or notches in; to create a nick{2} in, deliberately or accidentally; as, to nick the rim of a teacup.
quotes: And thence proceed to nicking sashes.
The itch of his affection should not then Have nicked his captainship.

defs: To suit or fit into, as by a correspondence of nicks; to tally with.
quotes: Words nicking and resembling one another are applicable to different significations.

defs: To hit at, or in, the nick; to touch rightly; to strike at the precise point or time.
quotes: The just season of doing things must be nicked, and all accidents improved.

defs: To make a cross cut or cuts on the under side of (the tail of a horse, in order to make him carry it higher).

quotes: For Warbeck, as you nick him, came to me.
